title: US carries out fresh strikes against Iran after tanker struck in Hormuz, escalating hostilities
gist: The US struck Iran again after a Panama-flagged tanker was hit in the Strait of Hormuz, the worst escalation since the two-week-old interim peace deal; Iran is reasserting control over Hormuz by demanding permits and warning shots, while oil has tumbled near pre-war levels on the supply released as blockaded ships exit.

insights.claim: Iran is reasserting control over the Strait of Hormuz by requiring ships to obtain Iranian permits before crossing, with the IRGC firing 'warning shots' at vessels using unapproved channels.
insights.speaker: Jared Szuba
insights.so_what: A permit regime turns Hormuz transit into an Iran-controlled chokepoint toll system, a persistent structural risk premium on crude and shipping insurance.
insights.quote: this was now prompting other ships to seek Iranian permits before attempting to cross the strait
insights.locator: But Iranian state television reported that the Revolutionary Guards fired

insights.claim: The US is promoting a southern Hormuz transit lane along Oman's coast while Iran wants ships on a northern route through its waters, where it ultimately aims to charge transit fees.
insights.speaker: Jana Choukeir and Jasper Ward
insights.so_what: Two competing transit lanes are the operational fault line; Iran's fee ambition is a durable rent-extraction motive that keeps Hormuz contested even post-ceasefire.
insights.quote: Washington has been promoting a southern lane along the coast of Oman, while Tehran, which ultimately aims to charge fees for use of the strait, wants ships to use a northern route through its waters and under its control.
insights.locator: Hundreds of ships, including tankers laden with oil

insights.claim: As blockaded ships began leaving the Gulf through Hormuz over the past two weeks, oil prices tumbled close to pre-war levels on the resulting surge in supply.
insights.speaker: Jana Choukeir and Jasper Ward
insights.so_what: Confirms the supply-release mechanism behind falling crude — a renewed blockade would reverse it sharply, defining the oil trade's directionality.
insights.quote: As they began leaving through the strait over the past two weeks, oil prices have tumbled close to pre-war levels on the resulting surge in supply.
insights.locator: Hundreds of ships, including tankers laden with oil

insights.claim: US Central Command struck Iranian military surveillance, communications, air defense, drone storage and mine-laying facilities in direct response to a drone attack on a Panama-flagged tanker, the worst escalation since the deal.
insights.speaker: Jana Choukeir and Jasper Ward
insights.so_what: Targeting mine-laying and drone facilities signals US intent to keep Hormuz open by force — escalation cycles around shipping remain the key catalyst.
insights.quote: targeted Iranian military surveillance, communications, air defense, drone storage and mine-laying facilities
insights.locator: U.S. Central Command said on Saturday

insights.claim: Each side accuses the other of violating the two-week-old agreement, with Iran citing the unfulfilled Lebanon ceasefire and US support for 'proxy forces' as breaches.
insights.speaker: Jana Choukeir and Jasper Ward
insights.so_what: Mutual-violation framing means the deal has no agreed compliance baseline, raising the odds of relapse and keeping the energy risk premium live.
insights.quote: Each of the warring sides has accused the other of violating the agreement reached two weeks ago
insights.locator: Each of the warring sides has accused the other

insights.claim: VP JD Vance, Trump's chief negotiator on the conflict, signaled a tit-for-tat enforcement posture: 'violence will be met with violence' while inviting Iran to use diplomatic channels.
insights.speaker: Jana Choukeir and Jasper Ward
insights.so_what: A stated reciprocal-violence doctrine caps the de-escalation runway; any shipping incident now carries a near-automatic military response, amplifying oil tail risk.
insights.quote: But violence will be met with violence

themes.theme: Lebanon as a parallel pressure point

insights.claim: Hezbollah leader Naim Qassem rejected the day-old Israel-Lebanon agreement as 'null and void' and a surrender, with anger spreading beyond Hezbollah to the wider Shi'ite community as displaced residents cannot return.
insights.speaker: Jana Choukeir and Jasper Ward
insights.so_what: Hezbollah's outright rejection means the Lebanon track is unstable and can drag the broader US-Iran deal down — a linked failure point for the regional risk trade.
insights.quote: rejected the day-old Israel-Lebanon agreement as a surrender, and said it was "null and void."

insights.claim: Israeli Defense Minister Israel Katz praised the Lebanon agreement specifically because it allows Israel to maintain occupation of a 'security zone' and bars displaced residents' return.
insights.speaker: Jana Choukeir and Jasper Ward
insights.so_what: Israel reading the same deal as license to stay, while Hezbollah reads it as surrender, guarantees the Lebanon ceasefire is built on incompatible interpretations.
insights.quote: saying it allows Israel to maintain its occupation of a so-called security zone in Lebanon and bars the return of displaced residents
insights.locator: Israeli Defence Minister Israel Katz praised the agreement
